final_answer: Bank of America (BAC 2024 10-K): The risk-factor disclosure on p.17 warns that 'elevated or rising U.S. interest rate levels' could result in additional currency volatility and recessionary conditions, and that changes in monetary policy can adversely affect operations. BAC's MD&A (p.29–30) and Selected Quarterly Financial Data (Table 7, p.35) show net interest income declined from the elevated levels of early-2023 to a 2024 trough — quarterly NII fell from $14,448M in Q1-2023 to $13,702M in Q2-2024 before recovering to $14,359M in Q4-2024 — reflecting deposit-cost pressure and asset-yield lag while short rates stayed high and the curve was inverted, which compressed BAC's net interest margin and weighed on return on average assets (~0.80–0.85% in 2024 vs. ~0.92–1.07% in 2023). BAC notes (p.51) it uses stress testing to evaluate balance-sheet/earnings impacts of such rate scenarios. Goldman Sachs (GS 2024 Form 10-K): GS's MD&A 'Operating Environment' (p.73) characterizes 2024 as dominated by macroeconomic concerns about inflation, central-bank policy, and uncertainty. The Net Interest Income discussion (p.74) states NII was $8.06B in 2024, 27% higher than 2023, because higher average interest rates lifted yields on trading assets, investments, collateralized agreements, and other interest-earning assets, partly offset by higher funding costs on collateralized financings, deposits, and other interest-bearing liabilities. The Distribution-of-Assets / Average-Rate tables (p.245) show the U.S. interest-earning-asset yield rising to 5.32% and total interest-earning yield to 5.18% in 2024 (vs. 4.77% in 2023); the Volume/Rate analysis (p.246) attributes most of the $12B+ increase in interest income to the rate component. Unlike a traditional bank, GS is less exposed to a classic NIM squeeze: only a small share of revenue is NII (FICC/Equities NII p.78), so prolonged high rates and an inverted curve raise both interest income and interest expense in tandem and primarily benefit GS's trading-asset yields, while the inflation/rate environment can still depress investment-banking activity and AWM performance (p.40, p.73). In short, prolonged high rates with an inverted curve compressed BAC's NIM and pressured profitability (NII trough mid-2024, lower ROA/ROE), while GS's NII actually grew 27% as higher rates lifted yields on its mostly market-based balance sheet, with the negative effects flowing instead through subdued M&A/underwriting and asset-management performance.

selected_chunks.content: Elevated or rising U.S. interest rate levels or high tariff rates, could result in additional currency volatility and recessionary conditions... changes in fiscal, monetary, regulatory, trade and/or foreign policy... could increase our compliance costs and adversely affect our business operations, organizational structure and results of operations.

Table 7 Selected Quarterly Financial Data: Net interest income Q1-Q4 2024 = $14,032 / $13,702 / $13,967 / $14,359M vs Q1-Q4 2023 = $14,448 / $14,158 / $14,379 / $13,946M. Return on average assets 0.83/0.85/0.83/0.80% in 2024 vs 1.07/0.94/0.99/0.39% in 2023; return on average common shareholders' equity 9.35–9.98% in 2024 vs 11.21–12.48% in 2023.

Management's Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations — forward-looking statements addressing future net interest income, provision for credit losses, expenses, and economic conditions, including impacts of interest rates.

Stress testing develops a comprehensive understanding of potential impacts of on- and off-balance sheet risks at the Corporation... contingency plans... Financial Contingency and Recovery Plan... to enable us to increase capital and/or liquidity, access funding sources and reduce risk during a period of stress.

Net Interest Income. Net interest income... was $8.06 billion for 2024, 27% higher than 2023, reflecting an increase in interest income, partially offset by an increase in interest expense. The increase in interest income primarily related to trading assets and investments (each reflecting the impact of higher average balances and higher average interest rates), collateralized agreements and other interest-earning assets (each reflecting the impact of higher average interest rates), partially offset by a decrease in interest income related to deposits with banks. The increase in interest expense primarily related to collateralized financings and deposits (each reflecting the impact of higher average balances and higher average interest rates) and other interest-bearing liabilities.

Distribution of Assets, Liabilities and Shareholders' Equity — Interest-earning assets average rate 5.18% in 2024 vs 4.77% in 2023 vs 2.08% in 2022; U.S. asset yield 5.32% in 2024 vs 5.45% in 2023; Net interest income $8,056M 2024 vs $6,351M 2023 vs $7,678M 2022.

Changes in Net Interest Income, Volume and Rate Analysis — Year Ended December 2024 versus 2023: Change in interest income +$4,608M volume +$8,274M rate = +$12,882M total, demonstrating that higher rates drove the majority of the increase in interest income across U.S. and Non-U.S. interest-earning assets.

Operating Environment. During 2024, the operating environment was generally characterized by continued broad macroeconomic concerns, including concerns and uncertainty about inflation, ongoing geopolitical tensions, central bank policy and the potential outcome of national elections. In investment banking, industry-wide underwriting volumes increased compared with 2023... industry-wide completed mergers and acquisitions volumes remained below historical averages.

Inflation has had, and could continue to have, a negative effect on our business, results of operations and financial condition. Inflationary pressures in recent years have affected economies, financial markets and market participants worldwide... increased certain of our operating expenses, and have [affected] asset management and wealth management businesses.

FICC and Equities net revenues — FICC net interest income $2,981M in 2024 vs $772M in 2023; Equities net interest income $(296)M in 2024 vs $126M in 2023. 'The increase in net interest income within FICC for 2024 compared with 2023 reflected an increase in interest-earning assets.'
